food_subgroup: Fermented rice and coconut milk bowl pancake

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Appam. Nearest parent is a fermented rice-coconut pancake. A new concept covering appam (bowl-shaped South Indian pancake) is warranted.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fermented rice-based pancake with coconut milk (closest parent)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code exists for appam; A01XS used as closest parent for fermented rice-based pancake with coconut milk.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for appam as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of appam is 2 pieces (approximately 150 g), as commonly served at South Indian and Sri Lankan restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Appam — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code F05010001
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo Database under food code F05010001 (Appam). This is an exact match for the Singapore-served preparation.

gi_source.title: University of Sydney GI Database — fermented rice pancake category (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for appam exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 76 (high) is estimated from the fermented rice pancake category. The high GI is consistent with gelatinised white rice starch as the primary carbohydrate. Glycaemic load of 34.3 is calculated for a 150 g (2-piece) serving: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (76 × 45.1) / 100 ≈ 34.3. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 175 mg per 100 g; a 2-piece serving (150 g) contributes approximately 263 mg sodium.
hypertension.guidance: Appam is moderately suitable for individuals with hypertension; a 2-piece serving contributes approximately 263 mg sodium (~11% of the 2,300 mg daily limit), so portion control is advisable and high-sodium accompaniments such as salted fish curry should be avoided.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic index (~76) due to gelatinised white rice starch; glycaemic load per 2-piece serving is approximately 34.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit appam to 1 piece per meal, pair it with a protein-rich accompaniment such as egg or vegetable stew to blunt the glycaemic response, and avoid sweetened coconut milk which adds further rapidly-absorbed sugars.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content of 3.8 g per 100 g (5.7 g per 2-piece serving) from coconut milk; cholesterol is 0 mg.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of the saturated fat contribution from coconut milk (approximately 5.7 g per 2-piece serving); limiting to 1 piece and choosing low-fat accompaniments can help keep saturated fat intake within the recommended 20 g daily limit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Coconut milk provides medium-chain triglycerides (MCTs) with some evidence for anti-inflammatory effects; fermentation supports gut microbiome health.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Appam is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; the MCTs from coconut milk may offer mild anti-inflammatory benefit and the fermentation process supports gut health, though evidence remains preliminary.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Appam is a distinctive South Indian pancake originating from Kerala and Tamil Nadu, widely popular in Sri Lanka and served at South Indian and Sri Lankan restaurants in Singapore. It is made from a fermented batter of soaked and ground white rice blended with coconut milk, leavened with yeast or natural toddy, and left to ferment overnight. The batter is cooked in a small, rounded wok called an appachatti, which gives the pancake its characteristic bowl shape: thin, lacy, and crispy at the edges with a thick, soft, spongy centre. Coconut milk imparts a subtle natural sweetness and rich aroma. Appam is naturally gluten-free, vegan, and dairy-free. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides 164 kcal, 3.2 g protein, 4.5 g fat (of which 3.8 g is saturated fat from coconut milk), and 28.5 g carbohydrate. The glycaemic index is high (~76) due to gelatinised white rice starch, making portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. In Singapore, appam is typically served as a breakfast or tiffin item accompanied by sweetened coconut milk, vegetable stew (ishtew), or egg. The fermentation process enhances digestibility and supports gut health.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo Database (food code F05010001), which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. This is an exact match for the Singapore-served preparation of appam. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and consistent with the known composition of fermented rice and coconut milk pancakes.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for appam is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo Database (food code F05010001) as of 2026-04-13. water_g set to -1: moisture content is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo Database for this food code. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as confirmed by HPB source (no trans fat detected). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as confirmed by HPB source (appam contains no animal-derived cholesterol). vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b12_mcg, and vitamin_d_mcg are 0.0 as confirmed by HPB source (no animal-derived or fortified sources present in standard appam). GI value of 76 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database fermented rice pancake category; no direct GI measurement for appam exists in the database. Hindi local name 'अप्पम' is a standard transliteration of the Tamil/Malayalam term into Devanagari script, consistent with usage in Hindi-speaking communities in Singapore.
